longTailKeywords: how long does acute bronchitis last

h1Title: Acute Bronchitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute bronchitis is a self-limited lower respiratory tract inflammation of the bronchial airways, almost always caused by viral infection, producing a persistent cough — with or without sputum — lasting up to three weeks. It accounts for an estimated 10 million outpatient visits per year in the United States and is one of the top five reasons adults seek primary care. Viruses — most commonly rhinovirus, coronavirus, influenza, and parainfluenza — cause more than 90% of cases; bacterial causes are uncommon in otherwise healthy adults. Despite this, antibiotics are prescribed in over 50-70% of visits, contributing substantially to antibiotic resistance without improving outcomes. Treatment is supportive: symptom relief with cough suppressants, bronchodilators when wheeze is present, and patient education about the expected duration.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 5% of adults report an episode of acute bronchitis each year; accounts for roughly 10 million physician visits per year in the US (CDC estimate)
keyStats.demographics: Affects all age groups; most common in children and adults over 50; risk increases in smokers, those with COPD, and during winter respiratory virus season
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in adults aged 20-40 and children under 5; second peak in adults over 65 with underlying lung disease
keyStats.globalCases: Acute bronchitis and related lower respiratory tract infections collectively cause an estimated 350 million episodes globally per year (GBD 2019)

definition: Acute bronchitis (ICD-10: J20.9 for unspecified etiology) is an inflammatory condition of the large and medium bronchi causing a self-limited acute cough lasting up to 3 weeks, frequently with sputum production, low-grade fever, and mild constitutional symptoms, in a patient without pre-existing chronic lung disease or radiographic evidence of pneumonia. More than 90% of cases in healthy adults are caused by respiratory viruses; in order of frequency: rhinovirus, coronavirus (including SARS-CoV-2), influenza A and B, parainfluenza virus, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), and adenovirus. Bacterial pathogens — Bordetella pertussis, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydia pneumoniae — account for fewer than 10% of cases but should be considered in atypical presentations, community outbreaks, or cough lasting beyond three weeks. Inflammation of the bronchial mucosa causes mucosal edema, increased mucus secretion, and ciliary dysfunction, all of which produce the cardinal symptom of cough and expectoration. The diagnosis is clinical; chest radiography is reserved for patients where pneumonia is suspected based on abnormal vital signs or examination findings. Pulmonology and general practice manage this condition, though most cases are handled entirely in primary care.

typesClassification.description: The predominant form, caused by rhinovirus, influenza, coronavirus, parainfluenza, RSV, or adenovirus. Cough begins with or shortly after upper respiratory symptoms (sore throat, runny nose) and lasts 7-21 days. No antibiotic treatment required. Self-limiting.

Caused by Bordetella pertussis, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, or Chlamydia pneumoniae. Often presents with a more prolonged cough (>3 weeks), paroxysmal nature (pertussis), or extrapulmonary features. Antibiotic treatment is indicated and effective.

Caused by inhalation of smoke, dust, toxic fumes, or chemical agents. Not infectious but produces the same clinical picture of bronchial inflammation and cough. Important in occupational health settings.

primarySymptoms: Persistent cough lasting 1-3 weeks — the defining symptom. Initially dry and hacking, often becoming productive with clear, yellow, or green sputum as the inflammatory phase progresses. Sputum color does not reliably indicate bacterial infection in acute bronchitis.

Low-grade fever (37.5-38.5°C) in the first 2-3 days of illness, more common when a specific viral pathogen such as influenza is responsible. High fever (above 39°C) suggests influenza or bacterial superinfection rather than typical viral bronchitis.
Chest tightness, discomfort, or substernal burning from bronchial mucosal inflammation, particularly worsened by coughing bouts.

Wheeze or rhonchi on chest auscultation in up to 40% of patients, caused by transient bronchospasm from bronchial inflammation rather than permanent airflow obstruction — this is termed 'bronchial hyper-reactivity' and typically resolves within 2-3 weeks.
Malaise, fatigue, and muscle aches accompanying the acute infectious phase, more pronounced with influenza-associated bronchitis.
Runny nose, sore throat, and mild headache as concurrent upper respiratory tract symptoms preceding or accompanying the lower respiratory symptoms in most viral cases.

Dyspnea on moderate exertion during the acute phase, reflecting increased airway resistance from mucosal edema and secretions. Dyspnea at rest or with minimal activity warrants assessment to exclude pneumonia.
Nocturnal cough that disrupts sleep, caused by increased bronchial secretions pooling in the recumbent position and post-nasal drip irritating the larynx and upper bronchi.
earlyWarningSigns: Onset of cough within 1-3 days of an upper respiratory infection — this sequence (head cold then chest symptoms) is the typical viral bronchitis pattern and helps distinguish it from de novo lower respiratory infection.
Mild wheeze or chest tightness in the first few days, which in an otherwise healthy adult suggests transient bronchospasm from viral bronchitis rather than new asthma.
Progressive cough more than 5 days after an apparent cold without symptom improvement — this may signal bronchitis developing rather than simple viral upper respiratory infection.

Yellow or green sputum appearing 5-10 days into an illness — a common and expected finding in viral bronchitis caused by neutrophilic inflammatory cells in the sputum, not necessarily bacterial infection.
emergencySigns: Respiratory rate above 24 breaths per minute or oxygen saturation below 94% on room air — these vital sign abnormalities signal pneumonia or a more severe lower respiratory illness requiring urgent assessment and chest X-ray.
High fever above 39°C with rigors, pleuritic chest pain, or dullness on chest percussion — features suggesting bacterial pneumonia rather than bronchitis; requires same-day medical assessment.
Severe difficulty breathing at rest or cyanosis (blue lips or fingernails) — indicates acute respiratory failure requiring emergency care.

Blood-streaked or frank hemoptysis beyond minor streaking — although trivial hemoptysis can occur with severe coughing in bronchitis, significant blood in sputum warrants urgent assessment to exclude pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, or malignancy.

causes.description: The most common cause of acute bronchitis in adults, accounting for approximately 25-30% of cases. Rhinovirus infects the upper and lower respiratory epithelium, triggering bronchial inflammation through direct cytopathic effect and host immune activation. Transmission via respiratory droplets and hand-to-face contact.

causes.cause: Influenza A and B virus infection
causes.description: Influenza-associated bronchitis is typically more severe than other viral bronchitis, with higher fever, more marked myalgia, and greater risk of bacterial superinfection. Annual influenza vaccination reduces bronchitis from influenza substantially. Incidence peaks in winter months.

Coronaviruses — both endemic cold-causing strains (HCoV-229E, HCoV-OC43) and SARS-CoV-2 — cause acute bronchitis as part of their respiratory illness spectrum. SARS-CoV-2 bronchitis can progress to severe pneumonia in a minority, but most COVID-19 cases in vaccinated adults manifest as bronchitis-level lower respiratory illness.

Pertussis causes a prolonged paroxysmal cough that can be misdiagnosed as protracted acute bronchitis in adults and adolescents who lack childhood immunity. The characteristic 'whoop' is often absent in adults. Diagnosis requires PCR or culture of nasopharyngeal specimens; macrolide antibiotic treatment is effective and reduces transmission.

Parainfluenza types 1-3 and RSV are common causes of autumn and winter bronchitis in adults, particularly older adults and those with underlying lung disease. RSV in adults over 65 or with COPD carries risk of progression to pneumonia comparable to influenza.

Tobacco smoke is the most important non-infectious cause of recurrent acute bronchitis. Smokers experience acute bronchitic episodes more frequently, more severely, and for longer than non-smokers, because baseline mucociliary dysfunction impairs clearance of viral pathogens and secretions.

riskFactors.description: Smokers have 2-3 times the incidence of acute bronchitis compared with non-smokers. Tobacco smoke impairs mucociliary clearance, damages bronchial epithelium, and reduces innate immune defence, prolonging viral replication and recovery.

COPD patients experience 2-3 acute exacerbations per year on average, many triggered by viral bronchitis. Acute bronchitis in COPD is more severe, more likely to require hospitalization, and more likely to accelerate lung function decline.
Young children have immature mucosal immunity and frequent viral exposure in nursery settings. Older adults have declining mucosal IgA and ciliary function, making bronchial clearance less effective.
Respiratory viruses circulate predominantly in autumn and winter in temperate climates. Low humidity, cold air, and crowded indoor environments facilitate transmission and impair mucosal defenses.
Annual influenza vaccination reduces influenza-associated acute bronchitis episodes by approximately 50-60% in community-dwelling adults and by 70-80% in years with good vaccine-strain match.
Patients on chemotherapy, corticosteroids, or with HIV are at risk of atypical viral bronchitis from pathogens like CMV and herpes simplex, longer illness duration, and progression to pneumonia.

Viral respiratory infections are the dominant trigger of acute asthma exacerbations in children and adults. People with asthma develop more prolonged bronchial hyper-reactivity after viral bronchitis than non-asthmatic individuals.

affectedDemographics: Young adults aged 20-40 develop the highest number of acute bronchitis episodes due to frequency of viral exposure at work and in social settings.
Children under 5 years are frequently affected; respiratory syncytial virus and parainfluenza are leading pathogens in this age group.
Adults over 65, particularly those with COPD or other chronic lung disease, experience more severe episodes with higher hospitalization rates.
Tobacco smokers of all ages; up to 15% of smokers visit a doctor each year with acute bronchitis.
diagnosisOverview: Acute bronchitis is a clinical diagnosis. The diagnosis is established by the presence of an acute cough of up to 3 weeks' duration, with or without sputum, in a patient whose history and physical examination do not suggest pneumonia. The key diagnostic task is to exclude pneumonia — the main alternative diagnosis — because the two conditions require different management. Vital signs are the most critical screen: a heart rate above 100 bpm, respiratory rate above 24 breaths per minute, temperature above 38°C, or oxygen saturation below 95% should prompt consideration of pneumonia and chest X-ray. The Diehr prediction rule and similar clinical decision tools can guide the decision to X-ray: normal vital signs, the absence of egophony, and the absence of dullness to percussion reliably exclude pneumonia without imaging in low-risk patients. Sputum culture is not recommended in uncomplicated acute bronchitis — culture results do not change management in the vast majority of cases. C-reactive protein (CRP) testing is recommended in some European guidelines as a point-of-care test to reduce unnecessary antibiotic prescribing: a CRP below 20 mg/L makes bacterial lower respiratory infection unlikely; above 100 mg/L it supports antibiotic use. Procalcitonin has a similar discriminating role in secondary care. Testing for influenza with rapid antigen testing or PCR is appropriate during epidemic periods, since a positive influenza test may justify antiviral therapy and informs infection control. Pertussis PCR from a nasopharyngeal swab should be requested when the cough is paroxysmal or has lasted more than 3 weeks without explanation.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination and vital signs assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies patients at risk of pneumonia based on elevated temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, and reduced oxygen saturation — the key triage step in every presentation with cough
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Temperature, blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation measured at the clinic or emergency department. Takes 5 minutes. The most important initial step in every cough assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes pneumonia, pleural effusion, pulmonary embolism, or mass lesion when clinical findings suggest more than simple bronchitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 2-minute X-ray standing in front of a plate. Painless. Results typically reviewed within 30-60 minutes. Not required in patients with normal vital signs and no abnormal chest signs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Guides antibiotic prescribing decision; CRP below 20 mg/L supports withholding antibiotics; above 100 mg/L supports antibiotic treatment in a patient with bronchitis symptoms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A finger-prick blood test giving a result in 4-8 minutes in a point-of-care device. Widely used in European primary care to reduce antibiotic overprescribing.

diagnosticTests.test: Influenza rapid antigen test or PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies influenza A or B when antiviral treatment (oseltamivir) is being considered — most cost-effective during influenza season or in high-risk patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab inserted gently into each nostril for 10-15 seconds. Rapid antigen results in 15-20 minutes; PCR results in 2-6 hours. A positive result can justify oseltamivir within 48 hours of symptom onset.

diagnosticTests.test: Nasopharyngeal swab for Bordetella pertussis PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms pertussis (whooping cough) when cough is prolonged (>3 weeks), paroxysmal, or associated with post-tussive vomiting or characteristic whooping
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab taken via the nostril to the nasopharynx — briefly uncomfortable. PCR results within 24-48 hours. A positive result confirms pertussis and mandates macrolide antibiotic treatment and contact notification.

treatmentOverview: Acute bronchitis caused by viruses is self-limiting and does not require antibiotic treatment. The 2016 NICE guideline on acute cough (NG120), the European ESCMID guideline on lower respiratory tract infections, and the American College of Chest Physicians (ACCP) cough guideline all strongly recommend against routine antibiotic prescribing for acute bronchitis in immunocompetent adults. Antibiotics do not reduce the duration of cough, reduce the risk of complications, or prevent deterioration to pneumonia in viral bronchitis; they do substantially increase adverse drug effects, cost, and antibiotic resistance in the community. Symptomatic treatment is the mainstay: honey (one tablespoon three times daily) has Level I evidence for reducing cough frequency and sleep disruption in children and adults; cough suppressants such as dextromethorphan (15-30 mg every 4-8 hours) provide modest symptomatic relief; inhaled bronchodilators (salbutamol 100-200 mcg as needed via inhaler) reduce wheeze and dyspnea in the 40% of patients with bronchospasm, with a number needed to treat of approximately 6 for symptom improvement. NSAIDs and paracetamol control fever and myalgia. Patients should be explicitly counseled that the cough typically persists for 2-3 weeks — this expectation management substantially reduces re-consultation rates. Antibiotics are indicated in specific circumstances: confirmed pertussis (azithromycin 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg days 2-5); confirmed Mycoplasma or Chlamydia pneumoniae infection; influenza treated with oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily within 48 hours for high-risk patients; and acute bronchitis in patients with severe COPD, as the European GOLD guideline considers antibiotics for COPD exacerbations with purulent sputum and increased dyspnea.

medicalTreatments.name: Dextromethorphan (15-30 mg every 4-8 hours as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: Centrally acting cough suppressant that raises the cough threshold in the medullary cough center. Available over-the-counter. Most effective for dry, non-productive cough. Not recommended for productive cough where expectoration is beneficial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest cough reduction; NNT approximately 5-7 for meaningful symptom relief in RCTs of acute cough

medicalTreatments.name: Honey (1 tablespoon, up to 3 times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Widely studied for acute cough in children and adults. Randomized trials show honey reduces cough frequency and severity and improves sleep quality. Suitable for children aged over 12 months. Free of adverse effects and resistance implications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cough frequency by approximately 25-30% and improves sleep disruption compared with placebo or no treatment in meta-analyses (Oduwole et al., Cochrane 2018)

medicalTreatments.name: Salbutamol inhaler (100-200 mcg, up to 4 times daily as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-acting beta-2 agonist bronchodilator for acute bronchitis patients with wheezing or bronchospasm. Particularly useful for patients with underlying airway hyper-reactivity or asthma. Should not be used routinely in all acute bronchitis cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT of approximately 6 for symptom benefit in wheezy acute bronchitis; no effect in non-wheezy patients (Smucny et al., Cochrane 2006)

medicalTreatments.name: Ibuprofen 400 mg or paracetamol 1 g three to four times daily
medicalTreatments.description: For fever management and pleuritic chest wall pain from coughing. NSAIDs also have a mild anti-inflammatory effect on bronchial mucosa. Standard over-the-counter doses suffice in most adult patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective fever control within 30-60 minutes; analgesic effect reduces cough-related chest discomfort

medicalTreatments.name: Oseltamivir (75 mg twice daily for 5 days, started within 48 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Neuraminidase inhibitor for influenza-associated bronchitis in high-risk patients (age >65, immunosuppression, severe COPD, pregnancy). Reduces illness duration by approximately 21 hours and reduces the risk of lower respiratory complications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces duration of influenza illness by 21 hours (Jefferson et al., Cochrane); greatest benefit when started within 24-48 hours of symptom onset

alternativeTreatments.name: Pelargonium sidoides extract (EPs 7630)
alternativeTreatments.description: An herbal extract derived from African geranium root, studied in several randomized trials for acute bronchitis. Meta-analysis shows modest reduction in bronchitis symptom scores and faster recovery compared with placebo. Available as oral drops or tablets.

alternativeTreatments.name: Umckaloabo / Pelargonium root extract
alternativeTreatments.description: Same active compound as above (marketed under different trade names). The 2020 Cochrane review concluded there is some evidence of benefit for acute cough with EPs 7630, though trial quality is moderate. Suitable as an adjunct in patients preferring non-antibiotic options.

whySeeSpecialist: Most acute bronchitis is managed successfully in primary care. Pulmonology referral is warranted when cough persists beyond 8 weeks (chronic cough), when recurrent acute bronchitis suggests undiagnosed asthma or COPD, or when a patient with known COPD has a severe exacerbation requiring spirometry reassessment and optimization of long-term therapy.
doctorSelectionGuide: A general practitioner can diagnose and manage the vast majority of acute bronchitis episodes. If symptoms persist beyond 3 weeks or recur frequently, a pulmonologist or respiratory medicine specialist can assess for underlying airway disease. Online or telephone consultation is appropriate for otherwise healthy adults with typical symptoms in the absence of red-flag vital sign abnormalities.
hospitalCriteria: Pulse oximetry below 94% on room air requiring supplemental oxygen
Suspected pneumonia requiring chest X-ray and intravenous antibiotics
Severe COPD exacerbation with respiratory distress not responding to outpatient bronchodilators
Elderly frail patients unable to manage oral hydration and medication at home
Clinical suspicion of pertussis in vulnerable contacts (infants, immunocompromised) requiring isolation and contact tracing
keyFacilities: General practice and primary care centers — appropriate for the vast majority of acute bronchitis
Walk-in urgent care centers for same-day assessment when vital signs need checking
Hospital emergency departments for patients with respiratory distress or abnormal vital signs
preventionStrategies: Receive annual influenza vaccination — recommended for all adults aged 50 and over, and for anyone with chronic lung disease, regardless of age.
Wash hands frequently with soap and water or use alcohol hand gel after contact with infected individuals or common surfaces during respiratory virus season.
Stop smoking — tobacco cessation is the most effective strategy to reduce frequency and severity of acute bronchitis episodes.
Ensure pertussis (whooping cough) vaccination is up to date — booster doses are recommended for adolescents, adults in contact with infants, and healthcare workers.
Maintain good indoor air quality — use air purifiers, avoid wood-burning stoves, and ensure adequate ventilation to reduce airborne irritant and pathogen load.
lifestyleModifications: Rest adequately during the acute illness phase — sleep and reduced activity allow the immune response to resolve viral infection faster.
Maintain hydration with at least 2 L of fluids per day — adequate fluid intake keeps bronchial mucus less viscous and easier to clear.
Use steam inhalation or humidified air to soothe irritated bronchial membranes and ease expectoration during the acute phase.
Elevate the head of the bed by 10-15 cm to reduce nocturnal cough from postnasal drip and bronchial secretion pooling.
Avoid inhaled irritants during recovery — tobacco, vaping aerosols, dusty environments, and strong chemical fumes prolong bronchial inflammation.

recommended: Warm fluids — herbal teas, broths, warm lemon and honey — soothe the throat and maintain hydration
Honey (one tablespoon, up to three times daily) for direct cough-suppressant benefit evidenced in clinical trials
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, kiwi, bell peppers) — immune-supporting role in respiratory infections
High-protein foods (poultry, legumes, eggs) to support immune cell production during illness
avoid: Dairy products in large quantities if they appear to thicken mucus secretions — evidence is limited but some patients report benefit from reduction
Tobacco and cannabis smoke in all forms during and after illness
Alcohol during the acute phase, which impairs mucociliary function and immune response
Cold drinks or ice — may trigger bronchospasm in patients with bronchial hyper-reactivity during the acute phase

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is recommended during the febrile or acute phase. Once fever has resolved and dyspnea has improved, light walking or gentle activity can be resumed. Strenuous aerobic exercise should be avoided for 1-2 weeks after symptom resolution in patients who experienced wheeze or dyspnea, to avoid provoking exercise-induced bronchospasm during the recovery phase of bronchial hyper-reactivity.
dailyManagement: Use the salbutamol inhaler as needed when wheeze or chest tightness occurs — do not use it on a fixed schedule unless directed by a clinician.
Take honey at bedtime and in the morning to reduce nocturnal and morning cough.
Drink at least 8-10 glasses of water or warm fluids throughout the day.
Avoid over-the-counter antihistamines that dry secretions (first-generation antihistamines) — they impair expectoration and may worsen mucus clearance in bronchitis.
Contact your GP or urgent care if you develop fever above 39°C, increasing breathlessness, or cough lasting more than 3 weeks without improvement.
prognosis: Prognosis for acute bronchitis in healthy adults is excellent. More than 90% of patients recover fully within 2-3 weeks without specific treatment. The median duration of cough is 17-18 days from symptom onset; many patients are surprised to learn the cough outlasts all other symptoms by a week or more. Complications are uncommon in healthy adults — pneumonia develops in fewer than 2% of outpatient bronchitis cases. In patients with underlying COPD, acute bronchitis is a major driver of lung function decline: three or more exacerbations per year in COPD are associated with accelerated FEV1 loss of 30-40 mL per year more than in non-exacerbating patients. Bacterial superinfection (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae) can occur, particularly in smokers and elderly adults, and presents as worsening rather than improving symptoms after day 7-10.
recoveryTimeline: Upper respiratory symptoms (runny nose, sore throat) resolve in 5-7 days. Fever and myalgia resolve within 3-5 days in most cases. Cough persists in 50% of patients at 2 weeks and in approximately 20% at 3 weeks — this is normal and does not indicate treatment failure or the need for antibiotics. Wheeze and exertional dyspnea typically resolve within 2-3 weeks. Full return to exercise capacity occurs by 3-4 weeks.
complications: Pneumonia as a rare complication in approximately 1-2% of acute bronchitis episodes in healthy adults, more common in elderly, immunosuppressed, and smokers — suspect if fever persists beyond day 5 or dyspnea worsens.

Post-bronchitic cough lasting 4-8 weeks after apparent recovery, driven by persistent bronchial hyper-reactivity following viral mucosal damage — manage with inhaled corticosteroid if persistent and disabling.
Acute exacerbation of COPD triggered by viral bronchitis, accelerating lung function decline and requiring hospitalization in approximately 10% of COPD patients per episode.

Pertussis complications in unvaccinated infants: rib fractures from coughing paroxysms, subconjunctival hemorrhage, secondary pneumonia, and rarely encephalopathy — underscoring the importance of contact tracing when adult pertussis is diagnosed.
Bacterial superinfection with Streptococcus pneumoniae presenting as worsening fever and new radiographic consolidation after initial improvement — requires antibiotic treatment.

supportResources.name: CDC — Bronchitis (Chest Cold) Information
supportResources.description: US Centers for Disease Control patient guidance on acute bronchitis, antibiotic stewardship, and symptom management.

supportResources.name: NHS — Bronchitis
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service patient information on symptoms, when to seek help, and self-care for acute bronchitis.

supportResources.name: American Lung Association — Acute Bronchitis
supportResources.description: Patient education from the American Lung Association on acute bronchitis causes, treatment, and prevention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia involves infection of the lung alveoli rather than just the bronchial airways. It typically causes higher fever (above 38.5°C), a faster respiratory rate, reduced oxygen saturation, and focal signs on examination (dullness, bronchial breathing, crackles). Chest X-ray shows infiltrates in pneumonia but is clear in bronchitis. Pneumonia requires antibiotics; most bronchitis does not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma is a chronic condition with recurrent episodes of reversible airflow obstruction triggered by allergens, exercise, or infection. A first wheeze episode during a viral respiratory illness may be bronchitis or an unmasked asthma attack. Asthma is suggested by a personal or family atopy history, recurrent episodes, and spirometry showing reversible obstruction. Persistent wheeze beyond 4 weeks after acute illness warrants spirometry to exclude asthma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: COPD is a chronic progressive disease with fixed airflow obstruction on spirometry; acute bronchitis is a self-limited illness. A COPD exacerbation may be triggered by viral bronchitis, but patients have baseline chronic productive cough, dyspnea, and reduced FEV1/FVC. Acute bronchitis does not cause a fixed spirometric deficit. A smoker over 40 with recurrent bronchitis should be assessed with spirometry to detect undiagnosed COPD.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: The main differential diagnosis requiring exclusion; shares cough and fever but involves alveolar consolidation and requires antibiotic treatment.

relatedConditions.name: COPD
relatedConditions.relevance: Viral bronchitis is the most common trigger of acute COPD exacerbations, which account for significant morbidity and lung function decline.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Viral respiratory infections including bronchitis are the dominant trigger of acute asthma exacerbations at all ages.

relatedConditions.name: Common Cold
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute bronchitis often follows common cold as the upper respiratory viral infection descends to the bronchial airways; the two conditions frequently overlap.

faqs.question: What is acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Acute bronchitis is inflammation of the bronchial airways usually caused by a viral infection. It produces a cough lasting up to 3 weeks, sometimes with sputum, low-grade fever, and mild breathlessness. More than 90% of cases in healthy adults are viral and do not require antibiotics.

faqs.question: How long does acute bronchitis last?
faqs.answer: The cough from acute bronchitis typically lasts 1-3 weeks. Upper respiratory symptoms (runny nose, sore throat) resolve in 5-7 days. Fever usually clears within 3-5 days. About 20% of patients still have some cough at 3 weeks — this is normal and does not indicate a complication or the need for antibiotics.

faqs.question: Does acute bronchitis need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: No, in most cases. More than 90% of acute bronchitis is caused by viruses, which do not respond to antibiotics. Major guidelines including NICE and ESCMID recommend against routine antibiotic prescribing for acute bronchitis in healthy adults. Antibiotics are reserved for confirmed pertussis, Mycoplasma infection, or COPD exacerbations with purulent sputum.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: The main symptom is a persistent cough lasting 1-3 weeks, often producing yellow or green mucus. Other symptoms include low-grade fever, chest tightness, mild wheeze, fatigue, runny nose, and sore throat. Yellow sputum does not indicate bacterial infection and does not by itself warrant antibiotic treatment.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have bronchitis or pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Pneumonia typically causes higher fever (above 38.5°C), faster breathing, reduced oxygen levels, and abnormal findings on chest examination. Acute bronchitis has milder fever, normal oxygen levels, and a clear chest X-ray. Any new breathlessness at rest, shaking chills, or oxygen saturation below 95% warrants same-day medical assessment to exclude pneumonia.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Symptomatic treatment is most effective. Rest, adequate hydration, and honey (one tablespoon up to three times daily) reduce cough frequency. A salbutamol inhaler helps if wheeze is present. Ibuprofen or paracetamol controls fever. Antibiotics are not needed in most cases and should be avoided unless specifically indicated.

faqs.question: Is acute bronchitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Viral acute bronchitis spreads through respiratory droplets and hand-to-face contact, as does any respiratory viral infection. The patient is most contagious during the first 2-3 days of upper respiratory symptoms. Good hand hygiene and respiratory etiquette (covering coughs) reduce transmission to household and workplace contacts.

faqs.question: Can I go to work with acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Most people with mild acute bronchitis can work if they feel well enough, though good hand hygiene and covering coughs are important. If fever is present (above 38°C), staying home for 24-48 hours after fever resolves is recommended. Healthcare workers and those in contact with vulnerable individuals should follow stricter exclusion guidance.

faqs.question: What causes acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Viruses cause more than 90% of acute bronchitis — most commonly rhinovirus, influenza, coronavirus, and parainfluenza. Bacteria including Bordetella pertussis and Mycoplasma pneumoniae account for fewer than 10%. Tobacco smoke and inhaled irritants can also cause non-infectious bronchitis.

faqs.question: Can smoking cause bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tobacco smoke damages bronchial cilia and mucus-clearing mechanisms, making smokers 2-3 times more likely to develop acute bronchitis than non-smokers. Smokers also recover more slowly. Chronic cigarette use causes chronic bronchitis — a separate persistent condition defined as productive cough on most days for 3 months per year over 2 consecutive years.

faqs.question: How is acute bronchitis different from COPD?
faqs.answer: Acute bronchitis is a short-term illness caused by infection that resolves within 3 weeks. COPD is a chronic progressive lung disease producing fixed airflow obstruction on spirometry, caused by cumulative tobacco or smoke damage. A smoker with recurrent acute bronchitis episodes should have spirometry to check for underlying COPD.

faqs.question: Should I see a doctor for acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Most healthy adults with typical symptoms can manage at home with self-care. See a doctor if you have fever above 38.5°C lasting more than 3 days, difficulty breathing, oxygen saturation below 95%, cough producing blood, or cough lasting more than 3 weeks without improvement. Older adults, smokers, and those with chronic lung disease should have a lower threshold for medical assessment.

faqs.question: Does honey help with bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Randomized controlled trials and meta-analyses show that honey (one tablespoon, up to 3 times daily) reduces cough frequency and severity and improves sleep quality in both children and adults with acute cough. It is at least as effective as over-the-counter cough suppressants and carries no antibiotic-resistance implications.

faqs.question: Can children get acute bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Viral bronchitis is common in children and caused by RSV, parainfluenza, and rhinovirus. In young children under 2, bronchitis with wheeze may overlap with bronchiolitis. Antibiotics are not indicated for viral bronchitis in children either. Honey is safe as a cough remedy for children over 12 months old.

faqs.question: What is the difference between acute bronchitis and chronic bronchitis?
faqs.answer: Acute bronchitis is a short-term infection-related illness lasting up to 3 weeks. Chronic bronchitis is a component of COPD, defined as productive cough on most days for at least 3 months in each of 2 consecutive years. Chronic bronchitis is caused by cumulative airway damage, usually from smoking, and does not resolve without addressing the underlying cause.

metaTitle: Acute Bronchitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Acute bronchitis: short-term airway inflammation causing cough and mucus, usually viral. Symptoms, when antibiotics are needed, treatment and recovery reviewed.

sources.title: Smith SM, Fahey T, Smucny J, Becker LA. 2017. Antibiotics for acute bronchitis. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 6:CD000245.
Oduwole O, Udoh EE, Oyo-Ita A, Meremikwu MM. 2018. Honey for acute cough in children. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 4:CD007094.
Smucny J, Flynn C, Becker L, Glazier R. 2006. Beta-2 agonists for acute bronchitis. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 4:CD001726.
NICE. 2019. Acute cough (including acute bronchitis) — antimicrobial prescribing guideline NG120. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, London.
Woodhead M, Blasi F, Ewig S, et al. 2011. ESCMID guidelines for the management of adult lower respiratory tract infections. Clinical Microbiology and Infection 17(Suppl 6):E1-E59.
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Antibiotic Use for Upper Respiratory Infections: Bronchitis (Chest Cold).
